interactions.summary: The interviewer asks why post-Soviet American unipolarity worsened rather than stabilized; Jiang answers with Bretton Woods, the dollar-gold contract, Nixon's petrodollar and China deals, financialization, the 2008 crisis, China bailing out global demand, and the later U.S.-China trade war.

claims.claim: Jiang calls the U.S. dollar's reserve role America's major imperial innovation: it financed reconstruction and gave Europe and Japan consumers, but depended on gold discipline.
claims.temporal_scope: Post-1944 global order as interpreted in this interview.

claims.claim: After Nixon severed gold convertibility, Jiang says the petrodollar and China's cheap-labor export machine became the two supports of the new dollar order.
Jiang says Wall Street financialization bundled the world into a fragile derivatives system, and China prevented total collapse after 2008 by absorbing raw materials through infrastructure spending.
claims.temporal_scope: Financial crisis aftermath as narrated on 2026-03-19.

signature_moments.moment: China is recast as the emergency consumer that bailed out the world by turning printed money into skyscrapers and high-speed rail.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It is a sharp reversal of the usual China-as-threat frame: China first stabilizes the order it later challenges.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: The U.S. capacity to issue the world's reserve currency and finance reconstruction, imports, and imperial reach through dollar demand.

chronology_notes.note: This packet sets the sequence 1971 Nixon shock, petrodollar and China deal, 2008 crisis, China bailout, and Trump-era trade war as stages in dollar-order instability.
